title: CIL Industrial Engineering — Advanced Mock 13 (Conceptual)
pattern: CIL MT — 200 MCQ · 2 papers · 1 mark each · no negative marking

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Statement: Several states have announced drought relief packages. Conclusion I: These states are facing drought. II: Central government will also announce relief.
questions.solution: Only I follows

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Statements: All papers are files. Some files are folders. All folders are covers. Conclusions: I. Some covers are files. II. Some folders are papers.
questions.solution: Only I follows

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: In a 5×5 square grid, how many squares?

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: A man faces west. Turns 135° clockwise, then 45° anticlockwise. Which direction now?
questions.solution: South

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Find the next: AY, BW, CU, DS, ?
questions.solution: EQ

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Scores: P > Q, R < S, Q > S, T > P. Who scores highest?
questions.solution: T

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Statement: India's coal imports fell 20% this year compared to last year. Conclusion I: Domestic coal production has increased. II: Coal consumption has decreased.
questions.solution: Neither follows

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Statements: All fruits are sweet. Some sweet are sour. No sour is bitter. Conclusions: I. Some fruits are sour. II. Some sweet are not bitter.
questions.solution: Only II follows

questions.stem: In a 4×4 dot grid, how many squares can be formed?

questions.stem: Find the missing: AD, GJ, MP, ?
questions.solution: SV

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Five boxes stacked. Box 1 below Box 3. Box 4 above Box 1. Box 2 below Box 5. Box 3 above Box 5. Which is bottommost?
questions.solution: Box 2

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Statement: Coal production has grown 8% this year while renewable energy grew 25%. Conclusion I: India is moving towards green energy. II: Coal production is declining in absolute terms.
questions.solution: Only I follows

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Statements: All coins are notes. No note is currency. All currency is paper. Conclusions: I. No coin is paper. II. Some notes are not paper.
questions.solution: Neither follows

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Two concentric circles with radii 5 and 3. How many tangents can be drawn to both circles?
questions.solution: 2 direct + 2 transverse

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: A walks 25 m north, 15 m east, 25 m south. Another man B walks 15 m west from same start. B is in which direction of A?
questions.solution: West

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: A transport company must send 8 trucks. 3 are needed for emergency. Only 5 are available. How many combinations possible?

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Should mining companies be mandated to spend 5% of profit on local community development? Arg I: Yes, mining affects local communities. Arg II: No, it reduces profitability.
questions.solution: Only I is strong

questions.stem: A points to a photo and says 'He is the son of the only daughter of my father-in-law'. How is the person related to A?
questions.solution: Son

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Find the next: 2, 5, 10, 17, 26, ?

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Statements: Some apples are oranges. No orange is grape. All grapes are sweet. Conclusions: I. Some apples are not grapes. II. Some sweets are not oranges.
questions.solution: Both follow

questions.stem: Find the wrong term: 2, 5, 10, 17, 28, 37, 50

questions.stem: In a code, 512 means 'you are good', 231 means 'they are bad', 145 means 'good and bad'. Code for 'they' is:

questions.stem: Find the next: 2, 4, 16, 256, ?

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: workers / the / demanded / conditions / working / better
options: Demanded the workers better working conditions
Working conditions demanded the workers better
The workers demanded better working conditions
The workers demanded better working conditions
Better working conditions the workers demanded
questions.solution: The workers demanded better working conditions

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Correct: The committee have submitted its report.
options: The committee have submitted its report.
The committee has submitted their report.
The committee has submitted its report.
The committee have submitted their report.
questions.solution: The committee has submitted its report.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: plays / role / coal / in / energy / a / vital / production / India
options: Coal plays a vital role in India energy production
Vital role coal plays in energy production India
In India coal plays a energy vital production role
Coal in India plays vital role energy production
Coal plays a vital role in India energy production
questions.solution: Coal plays a vital role in India energy production

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Correct: She is the tallest of the two sisters.
options: She is the taller of the two sisters.
She is the tallest of the two sisters.
She is more tall of the two sisters.
She is tall among the two sisters.
questions.solution: She is the taller of the two sisters.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: sustainable / practices / mining / promote / must
options: Sustainable development must promote mining practices
Mining practices must promote sustainable development
Must promote sustainable development mining practices
Practices mining sustainable must promote development
Mining practices must promote sustainable development
questions.solution: Mining practices must promote sustainable development

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Correct: Hardly had he arrived than the meeting started.
options: He had hardly arrived than the meeting started.
Hardly had he arrived then the meeting started.
Hardly he had arrived than the meeting started.
Hardly had he arrived when the meeting started.
questions.solution: Hardly had he arrived when the meeting started.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: country / infrastructure / of / development / backbone / the / is
options: The backbone of country development is infrastructure
Development of country backbone is infrastructure
Country development backbone is the infrastructure
Infrastructure is the backbone of country development
Infrastructure is the backbone of country development
questions.solution: Infrastructure is the backbone of country development

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Correct: The furniture in this room are expensive.
options: The furniture in this room are expensive.
The furnitures in this room are expensive.
The furniture in this room is expensive.
The furniture in this room were expensive.
questions.solution: The furniture in this room is expensive.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: data / protect / essential / is / privacy / to
options: Essential is to protect data privacy
Data privacy is essential to protect
questions.solution: Protect data privacy is essential

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Correct: He is more smarter than his brother.
options: He is more smarter than his brother.
He is smart than his brother.
He is smartest than his brother.
He is smarter than his brother.
questions.solution: He is smarter than his brother.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: renewable / future / energy / is / the
questions.solution: Renewable energy is the future

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Correct: He is more cleverer than his brother.
options: He is more clever than his brother.
He is clever than his brother.
He is more cleverer than his brother.
He is cleverer than his brother.
questions.solution: He is cleverer than his brother.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: environmental / concerns / need / planning / careful
questions.solution: Environmental concerns need careful planning

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Correct: He is suffering with high fever.
options: He is suffering from high fever.
He is suffering of high fever.
He is suffering by high fever.
He is suffering with high fever.
questions.solution: He is suffering from high fever.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: mining / India / in / industry / is / a / important / sector
options: An important sector in India is mining industry
Mining industry is a important sector in India
Mining is an important sector in India
In India mining is important sector
Mining is an important sector in India
questions.solution: Mining is an important sector in India

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Correct: She is one of the most talented singer in the industry.
options: She is one of the most talented singer in the industry.
She is one of the talented singer in the industry.
She is one of the most talented singers in the industry.
She is the most talented singer in the industry.
questions.solution: She is one of the most talented singers in the industry.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: government / new / the / announced / policy / has / a / mining
options: The government has a new mining policy announced
Government has the new mining policy announced
The government has announced a new mining policy
The government has announced a new mining policy
Announced a new mining policy the government has
questions.solution: The government has announced a new mining policy

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Synonym of COPIOUS:
questions.solution: Abundant

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: P: Artificial intelligence is transforming industries. Q: Machine learning algorithms analyse vast amounts of data. R: AI-powered systems can make predictions and recommendations. S: Ethical considerations around AI are being debated.
questions.solution: PQRS

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: She ____ a keen interest in geology.
questions.solution: showed

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Synonym of PRUDENT:
questions.solution: Cautious

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: The evidence ____ against the accused.
questions.solution: testified

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Synonym of AMICABLE:
questions.solution: Friendly

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Antonym of ABSTAIN:
questions.solution: Indulge

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Synonym of OBSTINATE:
questions.solution: Stubborn

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Total Quality Management (TQM) emphasizes:
options: Quality as the QC department's sole job
questions.solution: TQM is an organization-wide, customer-focused, continuous-improvement culture.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In a product (line) layout, machines are arranged:
options: In the sequence of operations for the product
questions.solution: Product layout lines up resources in process order for high-volume, standardized output.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A product is used at 80 units/day with a lead time of 10 days. The reorder point (no safety stock) is:
questions.solution: ROP = demand rate × lead time = 80×10 = 800 units.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A simple moving-average forecast gives:
options: Equal weight to each period in the window
Weight only to the latest period
questions.solution: An n-period moving average averages the last n observations equally.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An assembly line has a cycle time of 4 min and runs for 480 min/shift. The shift output is:
questions.solution: Output = available time / cycle time = 480/4 = 120 units.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) minimizes the sum of:
questions.solution: EOQ balances annual ordering cost against annual holding cost to minimize total inventory cost.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Productivity is defined as the ratio of:
questions.solution: Productivity = output produced ÷ resources (input) consumed.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A line makes 600 units using 40 labour-hours. The labour productivity is:
questions.solution: Productivity = output/input = 600/40 = 15 units/hour.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which statement about Cp and Cpk is correct?
options: Cpk ≤ Cp, with equality only when the process is perfectly centred
Cpk is always greater than Cp
questions.solution: Cpk penalizes off-centre processes, so Cpk ≤ Cp; they are equal only at perfect centring.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Annual demand = 1000 units, ordering cost = ₹50/order, holding cost = ₹8/unit/yr. The EOQ is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The control limits on a Shewhart control chart are usually set at:
options: ±3 standard deviations from the centre line
questions.solution: 3σ limits give ~99.7% of points inside when the process is in control (low false alarms).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Reducing an activity's duration by adding resources, at extra cost, is called:
questions.solution: Crashing shortens critical activities at the least incremental cost to compress the schedule.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A PERT activity has to = 4, tm = 6, tp = 10 days. Its expected time is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The PERT expected time of an activity (optimistic to, most-likely tm, pessimistic tp) is:
questions.solution: PERT uses a beta-distribution mean te = (to + 4tm + tp)/6.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: With demand 2000/yr, order cost ₹40 and order quantity 400, the annual ordering cost is:
questions.solution: Annual ordering cost = (D/Q)×S = (2000/400)×40 = ₹200.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which comparison of system reliability is correct?
options: A parallel arrangement is more reliable than the same components in series
Series is more reliable than parallel
questions.solution: Series reliability is the product (lower than any part); parallel redundancy raises reliability.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which statement about exponential smoothing is correct?
options: A higher α makes the forecast react faster to recent data
A higher α makes it smoother
α has no effect on responsiveness
questions.solution: α ∈ (0,1); larger α weights the latest actual more, increasing responsiveness.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Fixed cost = ₹50000, price = ₹80/unit, variable cost = ₹50/unit. The break-even quantity is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A transportation problem seeks to minimize cost of shipping from sources to destinations subject to:
questions.solution: It balances supplies at origins against demands at destinations at least total cost.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: With α = 0.3, last actual = 300 and last forecast = 180, the next exponential-smoothing forecast is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Aggregate planning sets production levels over a horizon of about:
options: Several months to a year (medium term)
questions.solution: Aggregate (medium-term) planning balances capacity and demand over months.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The break-even point is the output level at which:
questions.solution: At break-even, contribution covers fixed cost exactly, so profit is zero.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A PERT activity has to = 4, tm = 5, tp = 14 days. Its expected time is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The simplex method is used to solve:
questions.solution: Simplex moves along the vertices of the feasible polytope to the optimum of an LP.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the CPM network shown (path 1-2-4 = A+B = 5+7, path 1-3-4 = C+D = 2+9), the project duration (critical path) is:
questions.solution: Path durations: 1-2-4 = 12, 1-3-4 = 11. The critical path is the longest = 12 days.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Assembly-line balancing aims to:
options: Equalize workload across stations to minimize idle time
questions.solution: Line balancing assigns tasks so station times are nearly equal, reducing bottlenecks and idle time.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In a linear programming problem, the set of all points satisfying the constraints is the:
questions.solution: The feasible region is the (convex) set of all solutions meeting every constraint.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: With demand 4000/yr, order cost ₹50 and order quantity 400, the annual ordering cost is:
questions.solution: Annual ordering cost = (D/Q)×S = (4000/400)×50 = ₹500.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Little's law relates the average number in a system (L), arrival rate (λ) and time in system (W) as:
questions.solution: Little's law: average inventory/queue L = arrival rate × average time in system.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The last three demands were 120, 130 and 140 units. The 3-period moving-average forecast is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Linear programming is a technique to optimize a linear objective function subject to:
questions.solution: LP maximizes/minimizes a linear objective under a set of linear equality/inequality constraints.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An X̄ (X-bar) control chart is used to monitor the process:
questions.solution: The X̄ chart tracks the central tendency (mean) of a process over time.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An activity's normal time/cost are 8 days/₹4000 and crash time/cost are 4 days/₹8000. Its crash cost slope is:
questions.solution: Cost slope = (crash − normal cost)/(normal − crash time) = (8000−4000)/(8−4) = ₹1000/day.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Normal time is computed from the observed time using the:
questions.solution: Normal time = observed time × performance rating (rating normalizes worker pace).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A worker's observed time is 4 min at a performance rating of 80%. The normal time is:
questions.solution: Normal time = observed × rating = 4×80% = 3.2 min.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Adding components in parallel (redundancy) generally:
questions.solution: Parallel redundancy means the system works if any one path works, raising reliability.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: PERT differs from CPM mainly in that PERT treats activity times as:
questions.solution: PERT uses optimistic, most-likely and pessimistic estimates; CPM uses single deterministic times.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: On the break-even chart shown (fixed cost ₹30000, price ₹100, variable cost ₹40/unit), the marked point where revenue meets total cost occurs at:
questions.solution: BEP = FC/(p − v) = 30000/(100 − 40) = 500 units, where total revenue = total cost.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An R chart monitors the process:
questions.solution: The R chart tracks dispersion via the sample range, complementing the X̄ chart.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: On the control chart shown (CL = 70, UCL = 79, LCL = 61), the highlighted point lying above the UCL indicates that the process is:
options: In control with only chance variation
Out of control (an assignable cause is present)
questions.solution: A point beyond the ±3σ control limits (UCL = 79) signals an assignable cause — the process is out of statistical control.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Standard time is obtained from normal time by adding:
questions.solution: Standard time = normal time × (1 + allowance fraction).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Two components each with reliability 0.9 in parallel give a system reliability of:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Component A (R = 0.95) is in series with a parallel pair B (0.8) and C (0.95). The system reliability is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Safety stock is held primarily to protect against:
options: Uncertainty in demand and lead time
questions.solution: Safety (buffer) stock cushions against variability so stockouts are avoided.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: On the control chart shown (CL = 60, UCL = 72, LCL = 48), the highlighted point lying above the UCL indicates that the process is:
options: Out of control (an assignable cause is present)
In control with only chance variation
questions.solution: A point beyond the ±3σ control limits (UCL = 72) signals an assignable cause — the process is out of statistical control.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Queuing theory analyses systems characterised by:
questions.solution: Queuing models study arrival/service rates to predict waiting times and queue lengths.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The process capability index Cpk, unlike Cp, accounts for:
options: Process centring relative to the specification limits
questions.solution: Cpk penalizes off-centre processes; Cp assumes perfect centring and uses only the spread.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A task has a normal time of 6 min with 10% allowances. Its standard time is:
questions.solution: Standard time = normal × (1 + allowance) = 6×(1 + 10/100) = 6.6 min.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Depreciation in engineering economics represents:
options: The decline in value of an asset over time
questions.solution: Depreciation allocates an asset's cost over its useful life as it loses value.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A process has USL = 110, LSL = 90, mean = 102, σ = 3. Its Cpk is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: If the performance rating of a worker is above 100%, the normal time will be:
questions.solution: Normal = observed × rating; a rating >100% (faster than standard pace) raises normal time above observed.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The just-in-time (JIT) philosophy aims to:
options: Minimize inventory by producing only as needed
questions.solution: JIT pulls production to match demand, driving inventory (and waste) toward zero.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: On the break-even chart shown (fixed cost ₹20000, price ₹100, variable cost ₹60/unit), the marked point where revenue meets total cost occurs at:
questions.solution: BEP = FC/(p − v) = 20000/(100 − 60) = 500 units, where total revenue = total cost.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 260 140" width="260" height="140"><line x1="30" y1="120" x2="240" y2="120" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="1"/><line x1="30" y1="15" x2="30" y2="120" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="1"/><line x1="30" y1="80" x2="240" y2="80" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="180" y="76" font-size="8" fill="#475569">total cost</text><line x1="30" y1="120" x2="220" y2="25" stroke="#2563eb" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="180" y="40" font-size="8" fill="#2563eb">revenue</text><circle cx="120" cy="62" r="4" fill="#dc2626"/><line x1="120" y1="62" x2="120" y2="120" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="1" stroke-dasharray="3 2"/><text x="110" y="134" font-size="8" fill="#dc2626">BEP</text></svg>
figure.caption: Break-even chart

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Acceptance sampling is used to decide whether to accept or reject a:
questions.solution: A sample is inspected and the lot accepted/rejected per the sampling plan.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For D = 6400/yr, S = ₹80, H = ₹4/unit/yr, the minimum total ordering-plus-holding cost (at EOQ ≈ 506) is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which statement about control charts is correct?
options: Control limits equal the specification limits
Points within limits always mean zero defects
Control limits are based on process variation, not on specifications
questions.solution: Control limits (±3σ) come from the process itself; specification limits come from the customer — they are different.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The Hungarian method is an efficient algorithm for solving the:
questions.solution: The Hungarian algorithm optimally assigns n jobs to n workers (one-to-one) at minimum cost.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the CPM network shown (path 1-2-4 = A+B = 5+8, path 1-3-4 = C+D = 2+5), the project duration (critical path) is:
questions.solution: Path durations: 1-2-4 = 13, 1-3-4 = 7. The critical path is the longest = 13 days.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In exponential smoothing, a larger smoothing constant α makes the forecast:
questions.solution: Higher α weights the latest actual more, tracking changes faster (but noisier).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Two components of reliability 0.9 and 0.9 are in series. The system reliability is:
questions.solution: Series: Rs = R₁×R₂ = 0.9×0.9 = 0.81.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The reliability of a system of components in series is:
options: The product of the component reliabilities
questions.solution: Series system reliability Rs = R₁·R₂·…·Rn; it is lower than any single component.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The variance of a PERT activity time is:
questions.solution: Activity variance σ² = ((tp − to)/6)²; path variance is the sum along the critical path.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: On the Pareto chart of defect causes shown (tallest bar = cause B), the principle being applied says you should first address:
options: Cause B (the tallest bar — the vital few)
questions.solution: The Pareto (80/20) principle prioritizes the 'vital few' tallest bars; cause B contributes the most defects and is tackled first.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 240 140" width="240" height="140"><line x1="30" y1="120" x2="220" y2="120" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="1"/><line x1="30" y1="15" x2="30" y2="120" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="1"/><rect x="45" y="35" width="28" height="85" fill="#2563eb"/><text x="59" y="132" font-size="9" text-anchor="middle">B</text><rect x="85" y="70" width="28" height="50" fill="#60a5fa"/><text x="99" y="132" font-size="9" text-anchor="middle">P</text><rect x="125" y="92" width="28" height="28" fill="#93c5fd"/><text x="139" y="132" font-size="9" text-anchor="middle">Q</text><rect x="165" y="105" width="28" height="15" fill="#bfdbfe"/><text x="179" y="132" font-size="9" text-anchor="middle">R</text><polyline points="59,40 99,60 139,85 179,100" fill="none" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="1.5"/></svg>
figure.caption: Pareto chart of defects

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which statement about the critical path is INCORRECT?
options: It is the longest path through the network
It is the shortest path through the network
It determines the minimum project duration
questions.solution: The critical path is the LONGEST path; calling it the shortest is wrong.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: With α = 0.1, last actual = 200 and last forecast = 280, the next exponential-smoothing forecast is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The basic fundamental motions identified by the Gilbreths are called:
questions.solution: Therbligs (Gilbreth reversed) are 17–18 elemental hand motions used in micromotion study.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: MRP (Material Requirements Planning) explodes the master schedule using the:
options: Bill of materials and inventory records
questions.solution: MRP nets requirements from the MPS, BOM and on-hand inventory to plan orders.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: On the control chart shown (CL = 50, UCL = 62, LCL = 38), the highlighted point lying above the UCL indicates that the process is:
options: In control with only chance variation
Out of control (an assignable cause is present)
questions.solution: A point beyond the ±3σ control limits (UCL = 62) signals an assignable cause — the process is out of statistical control.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 280 130" width="280" height="130"><line x1="20" y1="30" x2="260" y2="30" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="4 3"/><text x="262" y="33" font-size="8" fill="#dc2626">UCL</text><line x1="20" y1="70" x2="260" y2="70" stroke="#16a34a" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="262" y="73" font-size="8" fill="#16a34a">CL</text><line x1="20" y1="110" x2="260" y2="110" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="4 3"/><text x="262" y="113" font-size="8" fill="#dc2626">LCL</text><polyline points="40,68 70,60 100,75 130,55 160,18 190,72 220,66" fill="none" stroke="#2563eb" stroke-width="1.5"/><circle cx="40" cy="68" r="3" fill="#2563eb"/><circle cx="70" cy="60" r="3" fill="#2563eb"/><circle cx="100" cy="75" r="3" fill="#2563eb"/><circle cx="130" cy="55" r="3" fill="#2563eb"/><circle cx="160" cy="18" r="4" fill="#dc2626"/><circle cx="190" cy="72" r="3" fill="#2563eb"/><circle cx="220" cy="66" r="3" fill="#2563eb"/></svg>
figure.caption: Shewhart control chart

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The reorder point (under constant demand) is given by:
options: Demand rate × lead time (+ safety stock)
questions.solution: Reorder point = demand during lead time, plus safety stock if demand/lead time vary.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Fixed cost ₹30000, price ₹100, variable cost ₹60/unit. The output needed for a target profit of ₹10000 is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Johnson's rule is used to sequence n jobs through:
options: Any number of machines for profit
questions.solution: Johnson's rule gives the optimal job sequence on two machines minimizing total completion time.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which statement about EOQ is INCORRECT?
options: EOQ varies with the square root of demand
EOQ increases in direct proportion to annual demand
At EOQ ordering cost equals carrying cost
questions.solution: EOQ ∝ √D, not D; doubling demand raises EOQ only by √2.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Fixed cost = ₹50000, price = ₹80/unit, variable cost = ₹60/unit. The break-even quantity is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: ABC analysis classifies inventory items by their:
questions.solution: 'A' items have high value and tight control; 'C' items are low value with loose control.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An activity's normal time/cost are 10 days/₹3000 and crash time/cost are 5 days/₹5000. Its crash cost slope is:
questions.solution: Cost slope = (crash − normal cost)/(normal − crash time) = (5000−3000)/(10−5) = ₹400/day.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In acceptance sampling, a Type I error (producer's risk) means:
questions.solution: Type I (α): a genuinely acceptable lot is rejected; Type II (β): a bad lot is accepted.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Using 3σ control limits, the probability of a point falling outside the limits when the process is in control is about:
questions.solution: About 99.73% lie within ±3σ, so ~0.27% fall outside by chance (false alarm).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: On the inventory cost curve shown (D = 2000, S = ₹50, H = ₹5), the order size Q* that minimizes total cost (where ordering and holding costs are equal) is:
questions.solution: Total cost is minimum at the EOQ Q* = √(2DS/H) = √(2×2000×50/5) ≈ 200 units, where the two cost curves cross.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 260 140" width="260" height="140"><line x1="30" y1="120" x2="240" y2="120" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="1"/><line x1="30" y1="15" x2="30" y2="120" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="1"/><path d="M40 25 C 90 90 110 100 240 112" fill="none" stroke="#16a34a" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="60" y="40" font-size="8" fill="#16a34a">ordering</text><line x1="35" y1="118" x2="235" y2="40" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="190" y="55" font-size="8" fill="#dc2626">holding</text><path d="M40 60 C 110 30 120 30 235 95" fill="none" stroke="#2563eb" stroke-width="2"/><text x="120" y="28" font-size="8" fill="#2563eb">total cost</text><line x1="120" y1="48" x2="120" y2="120" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1" stroke-dasharray="3 2"/><text x="110" y="134" font-size="8" fill="#475569">Q*</text></svg>
figure.caption: Inventory total-cost curve

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Method study (motion study) is primarily concerned with:
options: Finding the time a job should take
Finding the best way to do a job
questions.solution: Method study analyses and improves work methods; time study establishes the standard time.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: On the Pareto chart of defect causes shown (tallest bar = cause A), the principle being applied says you should first address:
options: Cause A (the tallest bar — the vital few)
questions.solution: The Pareto (80/20) principle prioritizes the 'vital few' tallest bars; cause A contributes the most defects and is tackled first.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 240 140" width="240" height="140"><line x1="30" y1="120" x2="220" y2="120" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="1"/><line x1="30" y1="15" x2="30" y2="120" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="1"/><rect x="45" y="35" width="28" height="85" fill="#2563eb"/><text x="59" y="132" font-size="9" text-anchor="middle">A</text><rect x="85" y="70" width="28" height="50" fill="#60a5fa"/><text x="99" y="132" font-size="9" text-anchor="middle">P</text><rect x="125" y="92" width="28" height="28" fill="#93c5fd"/><text x="139" y="132" font-size="9" text-anchor="middle">Q</text><rect x="165" y="105" width="28" height="15" fill="#bfdbfe"/><text x="179" y="132" font-size="9" text-anchor="middle">R</text><polyline points="59,40 99,60 139,85 179,100" fill="none" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="1.5"/></svg>
figure.caption: Pareto chart of defects

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Time study is used to establish the:
questions.solution: Time study (stopwatch) determines how long a qualified worker should take at a defined pace.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In CPM/PERT, the critical path is the:
questions.solution: The critical path's length sets the minimum project duration; its activities have zero float.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Daily demand = 60, lead time = 4 days, safety stock = 50 units. The reorder point is:
questions.solution: ROP = (demand × lead time) + safety stock = 60×4 + 50 = 290 units.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In a process (functional) layout, similar machines are grouped:
questions.solution: Process layout suits low-volume, high-variety (job-shop) production.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An assembly line has total task time 48 min, 6 stations and a cycle time of 12 min. Its balance efficiency is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In an LP, the function being optimized (maximized or minimized) is the:
questions.solution: The objective function expresses the goal (e.g. profit, cost) as a linear combination of decision variables.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The balance (line) efficiency of an assembly line is:
options: Sum of task times ÷ (number of stations × cycle time)
questions.solution: Efficiency = Σti/(N·C); the complement is balance delay (idle fraction).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: On the Pareto chart of defect causes shown (tallest bar = cause C), the principle being applied says you should first address:
options: Cause C (the tallest bar — the vital few)
questions.solution: The Pareto (80/20) principle prioritizes the 'vital few' tallest bars; cause C contributes the most defects and is tackled first.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 240 140" width="240" height="140"><line x1="30" y1="120" x2="220" y2="120" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="1"/><line x1="30" y1="15" x2="30" y2="120" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="1"/><rect x="45" y="35" width="28" height="85" fill="#2563eb"/><text x="59" y="132" font-size="9" text-anchor="middle">C</text><rect x="85" y="70" width="28" height="50" fill="#60a5fa"/><text x="99" y="132" font-size="9" text-anchor="middle">P</text><rect x="125" y="92" width="28" height="28" fill="#93c5fd"/><text x="139" y="132" font-size="9" text-anchor="middle">Q</text><rect x="165" y="105" width="28" height="15" fill="#bfdbfe"/><text x="179" y="132" font-size="9" text-anchor="middle">R</text><polyline points="59,40 99,60 139,85 179,100" fill="none" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="1.5"/></svg>
figure.caption: Pareto chart of defects

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The break-even quantity equals fixed cost divided by the:
options: Contribution margin per unit (price − variable cost)
questions.solution: Q_BE = FC/(p − v); each unit's contribution covers fixed cost until break-even.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Fixed cost ₹30000, price ₹100, variable cost ₹70/unit. The output needed for a target profit of ₹20000 is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which statement comparing PERT and CPM is correct?
options: CPM is probabilistic; PERT is deterministic
PERT is probabilistic; CPM is deterministic
questions.solution: PERT uses three time estimates (probabilistic); CPM uses single deterministic times.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Activities on the critical path have a total float of:
questions.solution: Any delay on a zero-float (critical) activity delays the whole project.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A PERT activity has to = 4 and tp = 16 days. Its standard deviation is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A Gantt chart primarily displays:
options: Activity schedules against a time axis
questions.solution: A Gantt (bar) chart shows each task's start/finish along a timeline.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Two components of reliability 0.9 and 0.85 are in parallel. The system reliability is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which statement about an LP feasible region is correct?
options: The optimum lies at the centre
It is convex, and the optimum lies at a vertex
questions.solution: LP feasible regions are convex polytopes; an optimum (if it exists) occurs at a corner point.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In single exponential smoothing, the new forecast is:
questions.solution: Ft+1 = α·At + (1−α)·Ft; it is a weighted average favouring recent data as α rises.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: On the inventory cost curve shown (D = 2000, S = ₹50, H = ₹4), the order size Q* that minimizes total cost (where ordering and holding costs are equal) is:
questions.solution: Total cost is minimum at the EOQ Q* = √(2DS/H) = √(2×2000×50/4) ≈ 223.6 units, where the two cost curves cross.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A Six Sigma process targets a defect level of about:
questions.solution: Six Sigma (with 1.5σ shift) corresponds to ~3.4 DPMO.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Customers arrive at 20/hour and spend on average 1 hour in the system. The average number in the system is:
questions.solution: Little's law: L = λ·W = 20×1 = 20.
